title: Precautionary Saving in a Financially Constrained Firm
abstract: For a firm that cannot raise external funds, cash on hand serves as precautionary saving. We derive a closed-form expression for the target level of cash on hand in the presence of persistent cash flows. Contrary to conventional wisdom, a mean-preserving increase in the volatility of cash flow can decrease this target. Over the set of admissible parameter values, the average impact of volatility on the target is zero. Endogenous selection, reflecting termination of firms that run out of cash, leads to a positive average impact of volatility on the target level of cash, consistent with empirical findings.

How much cash should a financially constrained firm hold to enable it to survive a period of negative cash flows? How does that level of cash depend on the variability of net cash flows from operations? These questions were first addressed byMiller and Orr (1966), who analyzed the cash management problem of a firm that faces stochastic cash flows.1This classic contribution derived a simple closed-form expression showing that the optimal average level of cash holdings is an increasing function of the variability of exogenous cash flows from operations. In the half century since Miller and Orr, models of the precautionary demand for cash by firms have become more sophisticated, and are often solved numerically rather than analytically. Most analyses support the original Miller-Orr finding that an increase in the volatility of exogenous cash flows will increase optimal cash holdings. In their review of the dynamic corporate finance literature,Strebulaev and Whited (2011)explain ‚ÄúThe central intuition behind the results in this figure is that any model feature that raises the probability of needing external finance in the future also raises the shadow value of cash and thus optimal cash levels.... Both variance and highly serially correlated shocks increase the probability of needing external funds for investment, so average cash balances are increasing in both of these parameters‚ÄĚ (pp. 105‚Äď107). We deconstruct this conventional wisdom and show that a mean-preserving increase in volatility can decrease optimal cash holdingswhen profitability is persistent. Indeed, optimal cash holding is a nonmonotonic function of volatility.

To demonstrate the departure from conventional wisdom, we model cash flows from operations as a two-state Markov regime-switching process in continuous time, with cash flowsŌēH>0in regimeHalternating with cash flowsŌēL<0in regimeLat random times governed by Poisson arrivals. To focus on a financially constrained firm, in our baseline model we put the financial constraint in its starkest form: we assume that it is prohibitively costly to raise external funds.2Therefore, cash flow from operations and any interest on cash held by the firm are the only sources of cash. The role of cash on hand is to function as precautionary saving that enables the firm to make payments required when it is in regimeLfacing negative cash flow. If the firm holds zero cash at any time when it is in regimeL‚Ā†, it is forced to terminate because it cannot make the required payments. The cost of termination is that the shareholders lose the continuation value of the firm associated with future cash flows.3The optimal payout policy‚ÄĒequivalently optimal cash management‚ÄĒreflects the tension between the incentive to retain cash in the firm as a precaution to avoid termination and the incentive to pay out cash as dividends to shareholders who have positive time preference.

In our parsimonious model, the only decision that the firm makes is the payout decision, specifically, how much cash to pay out as dividends and how much to retain within the firm as cash on hand. The optimal payout policy is to pay zero dividends whenever cash flow is negative or whenever cash flow is positive and cash on hand is less than an optimally determined valueX‚ąó‚Ā†. Thus, in regimeL‚Ā†, the firm draws down its cash on hand to make required payments, and in regimeH‚Ā†, the firm accumulates cash until cash on hand reachesX‚ąó‚Ā†. When the stock of accumulated cash in regimeHreachesX‚ąó‚Ā†, the firm begins to pay dividends at a rate equal to its net cash flow from operations, plus any interest earned on cash holdings, thereby maintaining cash on hand constant and equal toX‚ąó‚Ā†. The decision problem of the firm is simply to determine the optimal value of the target levelX‚ąó‚Ā†.

We examine mean-preserving changes in the coefficient of variation, denoted byőł‚Ā†, of the unconditional distribution of cash flow,Ōē‚Ā†. A mean-preserving increase inőłincreasesŌēH>0and reducesŌēL<0‚Ā†, thereby increasing|ŌēL|‚Ā†, which has two opposing effects onX‚ąó‚Ā†. The first effect is that an increase in|ŌēL|hastens the time when a firm with a given amount of current cash on hand will run out of cash if it remains in regimeL‚Ā†. The hastening of termination would seem to increase the optimal level ofX‚ąó‚Ā†, as a precaution against termination. The second effect is that the increase in|ŌēL|makes each dollar of cash less effective at avoiding termination and thus can reduce the optimal value ofX‚ąó‚Ā†. We define two critical values ofőł‚Ā†,őłA<őłB‚Ā†. For values ofőłin a right neighborhood ofőłA‚Ā†, the first effect dominates, soX‚ąóis an increasing functionőł‚Ā†. For values ofőłin a left neighborhood ofőłB‚Ā†, the second effect dominates andX‚ąóis a decreasing function ofőł‚Ā†. The second effect, which is novel, arises because an increase in|ŌēL|‚Ā†, not only reduces cash flow at the instant when regimeLarrives but also reduces cash flow as long as the firm remains in regimeL‚Ā†. This reduction in expectedfuturecash flow, which reflects the persistence of regimes, reduces the efficacy of cash as a precaution against termination.

Over the set of admissible values ofőł‚Ā†, the average impact onX‚ąóof an increase inőłis zero. Endogenous terminations reduce the population of high-volatility firms more rapidly than the population of low-volatility firms. Thus, ifőłis uniformly distributed across firms at the time of birth in regimeHwith zero cash on hand, heterogeneous endogenous terminations tilt the population toward low-volatility firms, for whichdX‚ąódőłis positive. Thus, the cross-sectional average value ofdX‚ąódőłis positive, consistent with empirical findings. This explanation suggests caution in interpreting a positive coefficient on volatility in regressions of cash on volatility. Even though our model can be consistent with such a positive coefficient, as just described, our comparative statics results show that for any given firm, a sufficiently large increase in volatility will reduce, rather than increase, the optimal target level of cash.

Throughout most of this paper, we focus on the level of precautionary saving measured in dollars. Specifically,X‚ąóis measured in dollars. However, precautionary saving can alternatively be measured in units of time. Specifically, how long will a precautionary holding ofX‚ąódollars of cash enable a firm to survive a continuous episode in regimeL‚Ā†, when cash flows out of the firm at rate‚ąíŌēL>0? The answer to this question isX‚ąó|ŌēL|‚Ā†. Remarkably, we show that this alternative measure of precautionary saving is, in our numerical illustration, a monotonically decreasing function of the coefficient of variation,őł‚Ā†. This finding is notable for two reasons. First, the monotonic relationship betweenX‚ąó|ŌēL|andőłstands in sharp contrast to the relationship betweenX‚ąóandőł‚Ā†, which is necessarily nonmonotonic over the range fromőłAtoőłB‚Ā†. Second, the finding that precautionary saving measured byX‚ąó|ŌēL|is a decreasing function ofőłis the opposite of the conventional wisdom that precautionary saving is an increasing function ofőł‚Ā†.

The original analysis of business precautionary demand for cash byMiller and Orr (1966)was conducted in a model in which cash flows are serially independent. Serial independence facilitated the derivation of a simple closed-form solution for the optimal target level of cash,X‚ąó‚Ā†, in our notation. In addition, serial independence allowed for the unambiguous finding thatX‚ąóis an increasing function of the volatility of cash flows. Since that original contribution, the literature has introduced various additional features of the firm‚Äôs problem, such as taxation, capital investment, opportunities for the costly issuance of debt and equity, and serial correlation of cash flows.Bolton, Chen, and Wang (2011)examines investment and cash holdings in a firm facing serially uncorrelated shocks. The discussion of a mean-preserving spread is limited to describing the concavity of the value function and does not address the impact on the optimal level of cash holdings. Also, in the framework of serially uncorrelated shocks,Hugonnier, Malamud, and Morellec (2015)examine optimal cash holdings and the bottom plots in panels A and B of their figure 6 shows that the payout thresholds of cash (the analogues of ourX‚ąó‚Ā†) are monotonically increasing in volatility.Gryglewicz (2011)shows analytically that an increase in the volatility of cash flow monotonically increases the target level of cash, unlike our analytic finding that the target level of cash is necessarily nonmonotonic in the volatility of cash flow.4

Some studies in the literature include serial correlation. The typical finding is that optimal cash holdings are an increasing function of cash flow volatility. For instance, the review of dynamic corporate finance by Strebulaev and Whited cited above allows for positive serial correlation of cash flow and concludes that optimal cash holding is an increasing function of volatility. The analysis in that review is a slightly simplified form of the model inRiddick and Whited (2009), which also finds a positive association of optimal cash holding and cash flow volatility in a framework with positively serially correlated cash flow. The upper-right panel of figure2in Riddick and Whited shows that the ratio of the stock of cash to assets is an increasing function of volatility.

Hartman-Glaser, Mayer, and Milbradt (2019)finds that the target level of cash on hand is nonmonotonic in volatility (see figure 1 in that paper). However, this nonmonotonicity is a consequence of agency costs in a dynamic contracting framework. When these agency costs are arbitrarily small, the target level of cash is monotonically increasing in volatility, as in the conventional result.

D√©camps et al. (2016)considers a model in which the cash flow process is the sum of a drift term and i.i.d. Brownian shocks. Both the drift and volatility of the Brownian shocks are proportional to the size of the firm, which follows a nonstationary geometric Brownian motion. As a consequence, the detrended value of a financially unconstrained firm is constant in the absence of financial constraints. In our model, the profitability shocks are persistent but transient, so the value of the financially unconstrained firm is regime dependent. The firm‚Äôs motive to accumulate cash is to survive a persistent but transient spell of losses. As in the literature on household consumption, the response of saving to transient-but-persistent shocks is different than the response to permanent trend shocks, and this is why our results differ fromD√©camps et al. (2016).5

Jiang and Pistorius (2012)analyzes a model with Markov regime switching in which profitability follows a different Brownian motion in each regime. That paper provides an efficient algorithm for calculating the payout thresholds numerically, but does not provide closed-form solutions for the payout thresholds. Our paper also analyses a Markov regime process for profitability, but with two important simplifications that facilitate analytic tractability: first, we focus on the case with only two regimes and second, we eliminate the Brownian shocks. With these two simplifications, we are able to obtain a closed-form solution for the payout threshold, perform comparative statics, provide explicit conditions under which it is optimal to pay dividends, and derive the stochastic distribution of cash. These contributions fulfill the goals of future research in the final sentence ofJiang and Pistorius (2012).6,7

Opler, Pinkowitz, Stulz, and Williamson (1999)finds empirically that firms in industries with higher volatility of cash flow tend to hold larger amounts of cash. However,Han and Qiu (2007)refines this empirical finding by emphasizing the distinction between firms that are financially constrained and firms that not financially constrained. That paper presents a model in which firms with higher volatility of cash flow hold higher amounts of cash if and only if they are financially constrained; for firms that are not financially constrained, there is no relationship between cash flow volatility and holdings of cash. Their empirical analysis confirms these different relationships for constrained and unconstrained firms. These findings suggest that a positive relationship between volatility and cash on hand is evidence of financial constraints. However, our analysis of precautionary cash holdings in the presence of a hard financial constraint finds a positive relationship for low values of volatility and a negative relationship for higher values of volatility.

Relative to the literature described above, our paper makes three major contributions. First, we derive a closed-form solution for optimal cash holdings, which, to our knowledge, is the first such closed-form solution in the presence of persistent shocks to cash flow. This closed-form solution facilitates the analytic description and interpretation of the behavior of optimal cash holding. Second, we show analytically that optimal cash holdings are nonmonotonic in the volatility of cash flows. Thus, an increase in cash flow volatility can lead to a decrease in optimal cash holdings. This finding is counter to the conventional result and we discuss the role of persistence of cash flows in this counterconventional result. Our analytic result provides an intuitive explanation. Third, we show that endogenous selection arising from heterogeneous survival probabilities of firms can rationalize a positive cross-sectional average impact of volatility on optimal cash holdings, consistent with empirical findings, even though this impact is negative for firms with high volatility. To our knowledge, this selection mechanism does not appear in the existing literature.

Finally, we note thatAbel and Panageas (2022)adopts the framework for dividends and cash management developed here. That paper applies this model extended to include capital investment. The focus of that paper is to interpret, within the context of a fully specified model of liquidity and capital accumulation, the estimated coefficients in regressions of investment on average q and cash flow. The contribution of that paper is to formally include financial constraints to deconstruct the estimated coefficient of investment on cash flow, and to show that conventional interpretations of this coefficient in terms of financial constraints need to be amended. The current paper has a different, more fundamental, focus, which is to reexamine the impact of the volatility of cash flow on the target level of cash on hand in the presence of persistent shocks to cash flow. To analyze this question, we do not need the additional modeling associated with capital accumulation. Even with this streamlined specification, the current paper is rich enough to offer nuanced and novel results about the relation between volatility and the target level of cash.

To analyze the impact of volatility onX‚ąófor parameter values outside this neighborhood, we set the interest rate on cash equal to zero beginning in Section4, where we present a global closed-form solution forX‚ąó‚Ā†. Section5shows thatX‚ąóis not monotonic in volatility. Subsection5.1demonstrates analytically that persistence is crucial for the novel effect that an increase in volatility can reduceX‚ąó‚Ā†. Subsection5.2presents the alternative measure of precautionary savings in terms of the length of time that a firm can sustain negative cash flow in regimeL‚Ā†. This alternative measure of precautionary savings is a decreasing function of volatility for all admissible parameters values. Section6analyzes the endogenous cross-sectional distribution of firms with different degrees of volatility and shows that endogenous termination of illiquid firms in regimeLtilts the population of firms toward those for which the impact of volatility on cash flow is positive. Sections7.1and7.2, respectively, show that our novel finding continues to hold in the presence of costly external finance and stochastic volatility of cash flow. Concluding remarks are presented in Section8.

article.7. Extensions:
7.1 Costly external finance

One of our major findings is that the optimal target level of cash on hand,X‚ąó‚Ā†, is necessarily nonmonotonic in the coefficient of variation,őł‚Ā†, first increasing inőłfor low values ofőłand eventually decreasing inőłasőłapproachesőłB‚Ā†, at which pointX‚ąóbecomes zero. Relative to existing literature, the novel part of this finding is thatX‚ąóis decreasing inőłfor some range of values ofőł‚Ā†. We derived our findings in a model with a strict financing constraint that prohibited any infusion of cash into the firm by any form of costly external finance. In this section, we briefly discuss an extension of the model, which is presented in AppendixE, that allows firms to acquire cash at a cost ofőļ>0dollars per dollar of cash raised. As we will report below, our major novel finding continues to hold with this extension.

With the ability to acquire costly external finance, the firm‚Äôs financing decision has two elements to it: first, how much of its cash flow from operations should the firm retain rather than pay out to shareholders (which is the element we have analyzed so far); second, how much external funding should the firm use. If the cost of raising external finance,őļ‚Ā†, is sufficiently high, then the firm will never use this opportunity, and the financing decision is identical to the one we have already studied. More interestingly, for lower values ofőļ‚Ā†, it will be optimal for the firm to acquire external funds in regimeLwhen its cash on hand is zero.28For sufficiently low values of the financing cost,őļ‚Ā†, the firm will rely exclusively on external financing to pay for the negative cash outflow from operations in regimeL‚Ā†; in this case, the firm would never accumulate cash on hand, that is,X‚ąó‚ąó=0‚Ā†, where we useX‚ąó‚ąóto denote the optimal target level of cash on hand when the firm has the opportunity to raise external finance. For intermediate values ofőļ‚Ā†, the value ofőļis not so high that it completely deters the use of external finance andőļis not so low that the firm relies exclusively on external finance; in this case, the firm will find it optimal to accumulate cash on hand up to a levelX‚ąó‚ąó>0by retaining cash from operations. In regimeL‚Ā†, the firm first uses its cash on hand to pay for required outflows. When cash on hand is depleted to zero, the firm will remain in operation by using external finance to pay for required outflows until the next arrival of regimeH‚Ā†.

In Proposition 15 in AppendixE, we show that for values of the coefficient of variationőłin a neighborhood to the left ofőłB‚Ā†, bothX‚ąó(the target value of cash on hand when there is no opportunity to use external finance) andX‚ąó‚ąó(the target value of cash on hand when there is an opportunity to use external finance) are close to zero. Over this range of values ofőł‚Ā†, bothX‚ąóandX‚ąó‚ąóare decreasing inőłtoward a value of zero whenőł=őłB‚Ā†.

7.2 Stochastic volatility

Up to this point, operating profits for a given firm were drawn from a time-invariant two-point distribution, so that the firm faces constant volatility of cash flow over time. Our analysis of the impact of volatility on the firm‚Äôs behavior can be viewed as a ‚Äúcross-sectional comparison‚ÄĚ of firms each facing different amounts of constant volatility. In AppendixF, we explore a ‚Äútime-series comparison‚ÄĚ for a given firm that faces different amounts of volatility at different points of time. Specifically, we assume that there are two volatility regimes, namely, a high-volatility regime with two possible values of cash flow (a high coefficient of variation), and a low-volatility regime with two other possible values of cash flow (a low coefficient of variation). The firm moves between the high- and low-volatility regimes in accordance with a Markov regime-switching process.

We show that when the coefficient of variation in the high-volatility regime becomes sufficiently large, the optimal target level of cash on hand is lower in the high-volatility regime than in the low-volatility regime. This counterconventional finding echoes the results in Section5.
article.8. Conclusion:
Firms undertake precautionary saving to protect themselves against unfavorable events that arise randomly. The conventional result is that a mean-preserving increase in the variance of random events will lead to an increase in the optimal amount of precautionary saving. We have reexamined that conventional result in a simple model of a firm that faces persistent cash flows. Our analysis has at least three contributions. First, we provide a closed-form solution for the optimal target level of cash,X‚ąó‚Ā†, held by a risk-neutral firm in an environment with persistent cash flows. We use this closed-form solution forX‚ąóto provide an interpretation of the magnitude ofX‚ąó‚Ā†. Second, and most substantively, we show that a mean-preserving increase in the variance of cash flows can actually reduceX‚ąó‚Ā†, which is the opposite of the conventional result. We provide an analytical, yet intuitive, explanation of the role of persistence in the counterconventional result. In addition, Proposition 9 formally states that in the limit as persistence approaches zero,X‚ąóis an increasing function of the coefficient of variation,őł‚Ā†, for all reasonable values ofőł‚Ā†; thus the counterconventional result depends on persistence. We also demonstrate that the counterconventional feature continues to hold if we replace the stark prohibition on external finance with the opportunity to use costly external finance. Third, we show that cross-sectional heterogeneity in the coefficient of variation,őł‚Ā†, leads to cross-sectional variation in the life expectancy of firms, which tilts the cross-sectional distribution ofőłin a sample of surviving firms toward firms with lowőł‚Ā†. This endogenous selection effect can cause the average impact of volatility onX‚ąóin a cross section of surviving firms to be positive, consistent with empirical findings.
